content.text: Language-specific client libraries — commonly called SDKs — wrap the raw HTTPS APIs exposed by LLM providers into ergonomic, type-safe interfaces that handle authentication, serialization, streaming, and error recovery automatically. For almost every agentic-AI project, an official SDK is the right starting point: it keeps application code focused on task logic rather than on HTTP plumbing, and it evolves in lock-step with provider features so new capabilities like structured outputs or tool use arrive as first-class typed objects rather than raw JSON.
An LLM SDK is a language-native package that exposes one or more provider APIs as method calls instead of hand-crafted HTTP requests. The
Python package, for example, publishes a
method that internally builds the JSON body, attaches the
header, posts to the correct endpoint, parses the response (including server-sent events for streaming), and raises typed exceptions on failure — all invisible to the caller.
The three most widely used SDKs in the agentic-AI ecosystem are:
PyPI / npm package; covers GPT-4o, o-series models, embeddings, images, and audio
package for Claude models; exposes the Messages API and tool-use blocks
package for Gemini models; replaces the older

Beyond first-party SDKs, gateway libraries like LiteLLM provide a single unified interface that routes calls to any of 100+ providers while still returning OpenAI-compatible response objects. Frameworks such as LangChain and LlamaIndex wrap SDK calls further to support retrieval, memory, and agentic orchestration.

Calling an LLM directly over raw HTTP looks straightforward until streaming enters the picture. Server-sent events (SSE) arrive as a chunked byte stream where each line may carry a partial token, a tool-call delta, or a lifecycle event. Parsing these correctly requires understanding the SSE framing protocol, reassembling deltas into coherent objects, and handling malformed or empty lines — several hundred lines of boilerplate before a single token is displayed.

Error handling adds further complexity. Providers return different HTTP status codes and JSON error shapes for rate limits, context-window overflows, content-policy violations, and service unavailability. An SDK encodes this knowledge as a named exception hierarchy so application code can catch
specifically rather than inspecting raw status codes.

API surfaces also change frequently as providers ship new features. The structured-outputs capability in the OpenAI API, tool-use in the Anthropic API, and caching parameters across both all arrived as SDK updates; applications that depend on the raw REST spec must track these changes manually, while SDK users simply update the package.
— auth headers, content-type negotiation, JSON encoding/decoding, and pagination are handled by the library, not the application
— request parameters and response fields are typed dataclasses or TypedDicts, enabling IDE autocompletion and catching typos at write-time
— both sync and async iterators abstract SSE parsing; callers iterate over chunks without touching bytes
— configurable exponential backoff for transient failures and rate-limit responses, preventing manual retry scaffolding
integrate with asyncio, enabling concurrent inference calls within an agent orchestration loop
— request/response lifecycle events can be captured via httpx event hooks or SDK-level callbacks for logging and tracing
— new provider capabilities (cached prompts, function calling schemas, vision inputs) appear first in the official SDK
— when a call fails in a non-obvious way, the stack trace passes through SDK internals; debugging requires understanding both the SDK and the underlying HTTP layer
— providers iterate rapidly; breaking changes (renamed fields, restructured response objects) appear across minor versions and must be tracked in
SDK tightly couples business logic to OpenAI's request/response shape; switching providers requires a rewrite unless a gateway adapter was used from the start
, and related packages; Lambda cold starts or edge deployments with tight size budgets may prefer a minimal HTTP client
— advanced scenarios like custom retry predicates or connection pooling require reaching through to the underlying
which is not always a stable internal API
— each SDK has its own shape for vision, audio, and image-generation; gateway libraries partially close this gap but add their own abstraction cost
, the SDK performs a sequence of steps that would otherwise fall to the developer:
— keyword arguments are validated against a Pydantic model or TypedDict; unknown fields raise immediately, not at the HTTP layer
— the validated Python object is converted to a JSON body with provider-specific field names (e.g., OpenAI uses
) is read once at client construction and attached to every request as an
for the actual TCP/TLS connection, respecting configured timeouts and proxies
— on receiving a 429 or 503, the SDK waits for the
header value (or an exponential-backoff delay) and re-sends, up to the configured
— for non-streaming responses, the JSON body is deserialized into typed objects (
); for streaming, the SSE byte stream is consumed through an iterator that yields delta objects
— non-2xx status codes are mapped to a named exception class (
) with structured metadata from the error body
The async path mirrors the sync path but uses
at the HTTP dispatch step and returns async iterators for streaming. The caller can run multiple async calls concurrently via
without spawning threads, which is the mechanism underlying parallel tool fan-out in multi-step agents.

The diagram below shows how user application code, the SDK layer, provider APIs, and optional gateway services relate to one another. The SDK layer is the boundary between developer-controlled code and provider HTTP endpoints.

attrs.alt: SDK architecture: application code → SDK layer → LLM provider API → optional gateway

content.text: The SDK layer absorbs all low-level concerns (auth, serialization, streaming, retries, error mapping). Application code only sees typed method calls and typed response objects. A gateway service, when present, sits between the SDK and the provider endpoint and adds routing, caching, rate-limiting, and observability without changing the application interface.
Building a new agent, chatbot, or LLM-backed service on a single provider
Streaming responses to end users — the streaming iterator saves substantial parsing code
Using provider-specific features (tool use, structured outputs, prompt caching) that are represented as typed fields
Running in an async framework (FastAPI, aiohttp) and needing non-blocking LLM calls
Targeting a production environment where built-in retries and error types reduce operational burden
Routing to multiple providers based on cost, availability, or model capability
Needing to switch providers without changing business logic
Running evaluation harnesses that test the same prompt across GPT-4o, Claude, and Gemini simultaneously
Building a minimal edge function where dependency size is tightly constrained — a single
call may be sufficient for non-streaming use
The provider offers a dedicated SDK with richer features (e.g., Anthropic's streaming event types are more granular than a raw httpx call reconstructed from scratch)
Working inside a framework (LangChain, LlamaIndex) that already manages the SDK client — instantiating a second client wastes connection pool resources

# Python — install the packages you need
pip install openai anthropic google-genai

# Node.js / TypeScript
npm install openai @anthropic-ai/sdk @google/genai
Store API keys in environment variables, never in source code. All official SDKs read their default key from a well-known variable name:

export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."
export GOOGLE_API_KEY="AIza..."
Instantiate a client once and reuse it across requests. Clients manage a connection pool internally; creating a new client per request wastes TCP handshakes and TLS negotiation time.

from openai import OpenAI
from anthropic import Anthropic

# Reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment automatically
oai = OpenAI(
    timeout=30.0,          # total request timeout in seconds
    max_retries=3,         # retry transient errors up to 3 times
)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment automatically
ant = Anthropic(
    timeout=60.0,
    max_retries=2,
)

response = oai.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "Explain TCP handshake in one sentence."},
    ],
    max_tokens=120,
    temperature=0.2,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

msg = ant.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    max_tokens=256,
    system="You are a concise technical writer.",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is a vector database?"}],
)
print(msg.content[0].text)
Use the streaming context manager to receive tokens as they arrive. This is essential for user-facing applications where perceived latency matters more than total latency.

import sys

with oai.chat.completions.stream(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a haiku about async Python."}],
) as stream:
    for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
        if delta:
            sys.stdout.write(delta)
            sys.stdout.flush()
print()  # newline after stream ends
Tool use lets the model request execution of a named function. The SDK returns a structured
that the application dispatches, then the result is fed back as a
Example 2 — Async parallel summarisation with OpenAI

Async clients enable true I/O concurrency: several LLM requests are dispatched simultaneously and results are collected when all complete. This is the pattern used by map-reduce agents that process large document sets.

import asyncio
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI()

async def summarise(text: str, index: int) -> tuple[int, str]:
    """Summarise one chunk; returns (index, summary) to preserve order."""
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise in one sentence."},
            {"role": "user",   "content": text},
        ],
        max_tokens=80,
    )
    return index, resp.choices[0].message.content or ""

async def summarise_all(chunks: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    tasks = [summarise(chunk, i) for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks)]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    # Re-sort by index in case gather returns out of order
    return [summary for _, summary in sorted(results)]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    docs = [
        "The mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell and generates ATP via oxidative phosphorylation.",
        "Kubernetes orchestrates containerised workloads across a cluster of nodes using a declarative API.",
        "Transformer models use self-attention to capture long-range dependencies in sequences.",
    ]
    summaries = asyncio.run(summarise_all(docs))
    for i, s in enumerate(summaries):
        print(f"Chunk {i+1}: {s}")

A customer-support automation pipeline processes incoming tickets asynchronously. Each ticket is classified, a draft reply is generated, and a confidence score is used to decide whether to auto-send or route to a human agent — all in a single async pipeline that runs up to 20 concurrent LLM calls.

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI(max_retries=4)

@dataclass
class Ticket:
    id: str
    body: str

@dataclass
class TriageResult:
    ticket_id: str
    category: str
    draft_reply: str
    confidence: float
    route_to_human: bool

async def triage(ticket: Ticket) -> TriageResult:
    # Step 1: classify intent
    cls_resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system",
             "content": "Classify the support ticket into one of: billing, technical, account, other. Reply with the category word only."},
            {"role": "user", "content": ticket.body},
        ],
        max_tokens=8,
        temperature=0,
    )
    category = cls_resp.choices[0].message.content.strip().lower()

    # Step 2: generate reply
    reply_resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system",
             "content": f"You are a support agent. The ticket is categorised as '{category}'. Write a polite, concise reply."},
            {"role": "user", "content": ticket.body},
        ],
        max_tokens=256,
        temperature=0.4,
        logprobs=True,
        top_logprobs=1,
    )
    choice = reply_resp.choices[0]
    draft = choice.message.content or ""

    # Use mean log-probability as a rough confidence proxy
    if choice.logprobs and choice.logprobs.content:
        import math
        avg_lp = sum(t.logprob for t in choice.logprobs.content) / len(choice.logprobs.content)
        confidence = math.exp(avg_lp)  # 0..1 range
    else:
        confidence = 0.5

    return TriageResult(
        ticket_id=ticket.id,
        category=category,
        draft_reply=draft,
        confidence=confidence,
        route_to_human=confidence < 0.7 or category == "billing",
    )

async def process_batch(tickets: list[Ticket]) -> list[TriageResult]:
    # Semaphore limits to 20 concurrent calls — respects provider rate limits
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(20)
    async def bounded_triage(t):
        async with sem:
            return await triage(t)
    return await asyncio.gather(*[bounded_triage(t) for t in tickets])

# --- demo ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    batch = [
        Ticket("T001", "I was charged twice for my subscription this month."),
        Ticket("T002", "The export button is greyed out in Chrome but works in Firefox."),
        Ticket("T003", "How do I change my registered email address?"),
    ]
    results = asyncio.run(process_batch(batch))
    for r in results:
        status = "HUMAN" if r.route_to_human else "AUTO"
        print(f"[{status}] {r.ticket_id} ({r.category}, conf={r.confidence:.2f}): {r.draft_reply[:60]}...")

The semaphore prevents bursting beyond the provider's per-minute request quota while still running the maximum safe concurrency. The client is constructed once and shared across all concurrent tasks, reusing the underlying connection pool.
Mistake 1 — Creating a new client per request

# WRONG: new TCP/TLS handshake on every call
def answer(question: str) -> str:
    client = OpenAI()  # <-- instantiated inside the function
    ...

# RIGHT: one client for the lifetime of the process
client = OpenAI()
def answer(question: str) -> str:
    return client.chat.completions.create(...).choices[0].message.content
Mistake 2 — Calling the sync client inside an async handler
Mistake 3 — Catching the base Exception instead of SDK errors
Mistake 4 — Pinning an ancient SDK version
Locking to a version released before a feature shipped (e.g.,
in Anthropic) forces either workarounds or silent failures. Pin to a minimum version in

[tool.poetry.dependencies]
openai = ">=1.30,<2"       # minimum that has structured outputs
anthropic = ">=0.28,<1"    # minimum that has prompt caching
Mistake 5 — Exposing the SDK client to browser-side JavaScript

in a React component or Vite bundle leaks the key to every visitor's browser. All LLM calls must originate from a server-side layer (Next.js API route, Express handler, serverless function) that holds the key in a server environment variable.
— share the instance across request handlers; configure timeout and max_retries at construction time
Use async clients in async frameworks
integrate natively with asyncio event loops
sized to 80% of your rate-limit quota to prevent accidental quota exhaustion
— pass the client as a constructor argument or function parameter instead of importing a module-level singleton; this makes unit testing trivial by swapping in a mock client
— the default httpx timeout is 5 minutes; set a sensible application-level ceiling (30–60 s for interactive, 120–300 s for batch) to prevent hung requests silently blocking workers
— all provider APIs return a unique request ID in the response or headers; log it with every call so operations engineers can link logs to provider-side traces
Pin a minimum version in CI
so security patches apply without a manual bump; run CI on both the minimum and latest to detect regressions
Abstract provider details behind a thin interface
method; swap implementations in tests without touching business logic

The dominant latency contributors in an SDK call are network round-trip time and the provider's time-to-first-token (TTFT). SDK overhead is measured in microseconds for serialization and is negligible relative to TTFT, which ranges from 200 ms to several seconds depending on model size and provider load.
Connection reuse is the single largest performance lever under the developer's control. An

maintains a pool of persistent TLS connections; reusing them eliminates the ~150 ms TCP + TLS overhead on every call. This is automatic when a single client instance is reused, and lost when a new client is created per request.

For throughput-bound workloads, concurrency scales proportionally up to the provider's requests-per-minute (RPM) limit. Empirically, running N concurrent async calls on an OpenAI tier-2 key (3 500 RPM for gpt-4o-mini) achieves near-linear throughput scaling up to the quota wall. Beyond the wall, retry-after backoff kicks in and throughput plateaus; the solution is to request a quota increase or spread calls across multiple API keys in different projects.

Streaming reduces perceived latency for interactive applications: TTFT drops to the time the first token is generated rather than the time the full response is serialized. For batch workloads where only the final answer matters, non-streaming is slightly more efficient because it avoids the SSE framing overhead.

Prompt caching, supported by Anthropic and available via a cache-control parameter in newer OpenAI endpoints, reduces both cost and TTFT for calls that share a large system prompt. The SDK exposes cache-control as a typed parameter; enabling it for stable system prompts longer than 1 024 tokens typically cuts costs by 50–90% on cached tokens.
— unified Python/TypeScript interface across 100+ providers; drop-in replacement for
SDK calls via a proxy or as a library; supports load balancing and fallbacks
— observability and routing gateway; wraps any SDK call to add caching, retries, A/B testing, and a cost dashboard
— open-source observability proxy; log and replay LLM calls without changing SDK usage
— HTTP gateway providing access to multiple models via a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint
— wraps provider SDKs as ChatModel objects; adds chains, memory, and retrieval
— wraps SDKs for document-grounded Q&A and structured RAG pipelines
— builds agentic loops (agent, tool, handoff) on top of the
package has first-class tool-use support that underpins custom agent implementations
Using LiteLLM as a unified gateway

# pip install litellm
from litellm import completion

# Call GPT-4o
response = completion(
    model="openai/gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Same code, different provider — no other changes needed
response = completion(
    model="anthropic/claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

The raw-HTTP approach is occasionally justified for serverless functions with strict bundle-size limits, but the manual SSE parsing required for streaming is error-prone enough that even in those scenarios a thin purpose-built streaming helper is usually worth the 5 kB of extra dependency.
Store keys in environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager); never in source code or
Scope keys to the minimum required capability (read-only where possible; separate keys per environment)
Rotate keys on a schedule and immediately on suspected exposure; most providers support multiple active keys to enable zero-downtime rotation
On the client side (browsers, mobile), never embed provider API keys; route all LLM calls through a server-side proxy that authenticates your own users
— the SDK's built-in retry is safe for idempotent GET-equivalent calls; for tool-call side effects, implement idempotency keys at the tool level, not the LLM call level
— wrap the SDK call in a circuit breaker (e.g.,
) to stop retrying a consistently unavailable endpoint and fail fast
— set an SDK-level timeout shorter than the upstream load balancer or API gateway timeout to receive a controlled exception rather than a connection reset
— configure LiteLLM or PortKey to fall back to a secondary provider (e.g., Claude → GPT-4o) when the primary returns 5xx errors, ensuring availability even during provider outages
in the OpenAI SDK or the
response header) alongside the exception so provider support can diagnose server-side issues

Provider safety filters can reject requests or produce refusals that look like normal text responses. Agentic systems that feed model output back as input for subsequent steps must distinguish an intended model output from a safety refusal. Inspect the
in Anthropic responses before treating the text as authoritative data.
Official SDKs handle authentication, serialization, streaming (SSE parsing), retries, and typed exceptions — using them is nearly always preferable to raw HTTP for production code
) are essential in async frameworks and for concurrent agent tool fan-out; the sync client blocks the event loop
Construct one client instance per process and reuse it; instantiating a new client per request forfeits connection-pool reuse and adds measurable latency
to distinguish transient from permanent failures
Gateway libraries like LiteLLM add multi-provider routing and observability with minimal code changes; they are worth the dependency overhead in multi-provider or evaluation workloads
API keys must never appear in client-side code, committed source files, or logs; all LLM calls must originate server-side with keys stored in environment variables or a secrets manager
Prompt caching (Anthropic cache_control, OpenAI cached inputs) can cut token costs by 50–90% for repeated large system prompts; the SDK exposes this as a typed parameter
Pin SDKs to a minimum version (not an exact version) in CI to receive security patches automatically while preventing accidental breakage from major-version API changes
